additional_stakeholders.involvement: Primary voice on requirements. Owns quoting, contracts, and subscription tracking. Decision maker on commercial tooling.
Initial point of contact. Owns HubSpot administration. Would co-manage implementation alongside finance.

next_steps: Schedule tailored demo for Thursday May 8 at 3:00 PM ET covering co-terming, renewal quoting, and price book setup based on prospect's Excel model
Include sales team lead in demo to cover full quoting workflow
Prepare total cost of ownership breakdown including implementation support

jtbd.job: Migrate subscription and ARR tracking from a finance-owned Excel spreadsheet into HubSpot so non-finance teams have visibility
jtbd.context: CFO built an Excel-based subscription management system four years ago when the company had 20 logos. Now at ~60 logos, only the finance team can answer questions about customer ARR, upsell history, and contract status. Customer success, sales, and marketing are locked out of this data.
jtbd.desired_outcome: Any team member can navigate to a customer in HubSpot and see current ARR, upsell history, contract anniversary dates, and renewal status without asking finance.

jtbd.job: Replace Google Slide deck quoting with a structured quoting tool that maintains quote history and version tracking
jtbd.context: Sales reps currently build quotes in Google Slide decks, manually changing numbers. There is no audit trail of what was quoted, when, or how the price evolved before signature.
jtbd.desired_outcome: Complete quote history tied to deals in HubSpot: which price was sent on which date, how it changed, and when the customer signed.

jtbd.job: Consolidate the product library into a single source of truth instead of maintaining it in two places
jtbd.context: Product library exists in both HubSpot and a Google Sheet pricing calculator. When prices change, the spreadsheet gets updated but HubSpot often falls out of sync. Multiple refresh audits have failed to keep them aligned.
jtbd.desired_outcome: One authoritative product catalog with pricing that both finance and sales reference, eliminating dual-maintenance drift.

jtbd.job: Handle co-terming of mid-contract upsells to contract anniversary dates in whole calendar months
jtbd.context: CFO standardized all contracts to whole calendar months specifically to simplify co-terming. When a customer takes an upsell mid-contract, it needs to be prorated to the next contract anniversary, then roll into the annual renewal at the escalated rate.
jtbd.desired_outcome: Automated co-term calculation at the month level that generates correct prorated upsell quotes and rolls them into anniversary billing with annual escalators.

pains.pain: Subscription and ARR data is trapped in a finance-owned Excel spreadsheet with no shared visibility
pains.trigger: Any time a non-finance team member needs to know a customer's ARR, upsell history, or contract status. Customer success, sales, and marketing must ask finance directly.
pains.cost: Finance team becomes a bottleneck for basic customer intelligence. Customer success cannot see subscription details when handling support tickets. Marketing cannot identify cross-sell targets without asking finance for a data pull. Scaled from manageable at 20 logos to unsustainable at 60.
pains.urgency_reason: Company has tripled its customer base since the spreadsheet was built. CFO explicitly identified shared visibility as the primary driver for this evaluation.

pains.pain: No quote history or version tracking because quotes go out as Google Slide decks
pains.trigger: When anyone needs to reconstruct the negotiation history of a deal: what was quoted, when, and how the price changed before signature.
pains.cost: Cannot audit pricing decisions retrospectively. No ability to track discount patterns or quote evolution. Deal desk review happens only at contract stage, missing earlier pricing issues.
pains.urgency_reason: Acknowledged pain but not the primary driver for evaluation. RevOps lead raised it as a secondary concern.

pains.pain: Product library falls out of sync between HubSpot and the pricing spreadsheet
pains.trigger: Every time a price changes or new offering is added. The spreadsheet gets updated, but HubSpot product line items do not. Multiple refresh audits have been attempted and failed to stick.
pains.cost: Sales may quote stale prices from HubSpot. Finance maintains the 'real' pricing in a spreadsheet. Dual maintenance creates ongoing reconciliation burden and trust issues with CRM data.
pains.urgency_reason: Chronic issue that has persisted through multiple attempted fixes. Not urgent on its own but compounds the visibility problem.

pains.pain: Low bandwidth for implementing new systems because the team is manually handling high volume with low staff
pains.trigger: Any new tool evaluation. The finance team and RevOps are stretched thin running manual processes. CFO quoted a colleague's expression: 'too busy firing my bow and arrow to learn to use your machine gun.'
pains.cost: Perpetuates the manual status quo. Known improvements get deferred because the team lacks implementation capacity. No IT department exists at the company.
pains.urgency_reason: Not blocking the evaluation but will influence implementation timeline and total cost of ownership expectations.

pains.pain: HubSpot native quoting cannot handle multi-year contracts with annual escalators, co-termed upsells, and anniversary billing
pains.trigger: CFO tried to use HubSpot natively but hit limitations immediately. Cannot set end dates for co-termed line items (only term length). Cannot display current-year ARR inclusive of upsells and escalators.
pains.cost: Forced the team to build and maintain the Excel workaround for four years. Prevents migration of subscription data into the CRM where it belongs.
pains.urgency_reason: This is the functional gap that makes the Excel spreadsheet necessary. Solving it unblocks the primary job-to-be-done.

pains.pain: No contract lifecycle management tooling. Contracts managed in Microsoft Word and Google Drive with manual processes.
pains.trigger: Redlining on initial contracts is frequent. Scope-of-work adjustments for upsells require manual coordination between sales, services, and finance.
pains.cost: CFO personally took over contract management because it was, in his words, a mess. Finance team reviews every contract before it goes out via DocuSign. Manual coordination across teams for every deal.
pains.urgency_reason: CFO acknowledged the need but explicitly scoped it out of this evaluation. Noted as a future initiative with bandwidth constraints.

workflow_reality.current_process: Sales reps build quotes in Google Slide decks, manually adjusting numbers. Finance maintains all subscription, ARR, and contract data in an Excel spreadsheet (Google Sheet). When a customer is ready to sign, the deal goes to deal desk (CFO and finance team) for commercial review and contract preparation in Microsoft Word. Contracts go out via DocuSign. HubSpot is used for deal tracking but quotes show only two rolled-up line items (ARR total and implementation total) rather than full product-level detail.
tools_in_use: HubSpot Sales Hub Professional (CRM, deal tracking, limited product line items)
Google Sheets (pricing calculator, subscription tracking, source of truth for ARR)
Google Slides (quote generation by sales reps)
workflow_reality.team_size_quoting: 4-6 sales reps plus deal desk (CFO and finance team member)
brittleness_indicators: Product library in HubSpot repeatedly falls out of sync with the pricing spreadsheet despite multiple refresh audits
Only the finance team can answer basic customer ARR questions — single point of failure for customer intelligence
No IT department. RevOps lead, CFO, and one finance team member would own implementation of any new tool
Quote construction in Google Slides provides zero audit trail, version history, or policy enforcement
Deal desk reviews contracts but not quotes — pricing errors can persist until contract stage
CFO working at 2:00 AM regularly, indicating severe capacity constraints

evaluated.name: HubSpot CPQ (native quoting)
evaluated.what_they_liked: Already included. CFO values out-of-the-box solutions and tried to use it.
evaluated.why_they_passed: Cannot handle co-terming, multi-year contracts with annual escalators, tiered pricing by AUM, or anniversary billing. Co-terming only supports term length, not end dates. Product described as 'okay' by the CFO. No contract object (coming in September 2026 beta).
evaluated.key_quote: It blows my mind that HubSpot doesn't have this natively.

evaluated.name: Excel spreadsheets (Google Sheets)
evaluated.what_they_liked: Flexible. CFO built it in tools he knows. Handles the full pricing model including co-terming, annual escalators, and multi-year contracts. Data is clean with well-defined rules.
evaluated.why_they_passed: Cannot share visibility beyond finance. Not scalable from 20 to 60+ logos. Not integrated with HubSpot. Requires navigating outside the CRM.
evaluated.key_quote: You get a lot of flexibility out of that. It just doesn't make it very scalable.

evaluated.name: Google Slides
evaluated.what_they_liked: Easy for reps to create and send.
evaluated.why_they_passed: No quote history, no version tracking, no audit trail of pricing evolution. Cannot reconstruct what was quoted and when.
evaluated.key_quote: Quotes are going out via a deck. Someone sending a Google slide deck, and then they're changing some numbers and sending it.

current_workaround.description: Finance-owned Google Sheet for subscription tracking and pricing calculations, Google Slides for customer-facing quotes, Microsoft Word for contracts, DocuSign for execution. HubSpot used for deal tracking with minimal product detail.
current_workaround.adequacy: Functional but not scalable. Works because the CFO personally built and maintains it with clean rules. Breaks down on visibility (only finance can access), auditability (no quote history), and dual maintenance (product library sync failures).

quotes.quote: If someone wants to know a customer's ARR and what upsells they've had and how they got there, I can navigate into a spreadsheet and show them in 30 seconds. But I can't give an end user that level of visibility.
quotes.usage_notes: Captures the core pain: data exists but is trapped in finance. Strong for content targeting the transition from spreadsheet to CRM-based subscription management.

quotes.quote: That worked when we had 20 logos when I came in. Now we're at about 60. It's kind of time to share the magic behind the curtain.
quotes.usage_notes: Perfect growth inflection point quote. Validates the ICP signal of companies outgrowing manual processes. Good for content about when to upgrade from spreadsheets.

quotes.quote: It blows my mind that HubSpot doesn't have this natively.
quotes.usage_notes: Direct validation of the HubSpot CPQ gap that Quotivity fills. Usable in competitive positioning content. Demonstrates buyer frustration with HubSpot's native capabilities.

quotes.quote: Quotes are going out via a deck. Someone sending a Google slide deck, and then they're changing some numbers and sending it. So I can't easily go back and say, on October 15th we sent them this price.
quotes.usage_notes: Vivid description of the broken quoting process. Strong for content about quote governance and audit trails. Relatable to any team sending quotes outside their CRM.

quotes.quote: You get a lot of flexibility out of that. It just doesn't make it very scalable.
quotes.usage_notes: Honest assessment of the spreadsheet tradeoff. Good for content that respects the status quo before making the case for change.

quotes.quote: I'm too busy firing my bow and arrow to learn how to use your machine gun.
quotes.usage_notes: Memorable metaphor for the implementation bandwidth objection. Directly relevant to speed-to-value messaging and low-touch onboarding positioning.

quotes.quote: A price changes, it gets updated in the spreadsheet, and maybe that doesn't make it back over. So we've used the product line items. However, it would need another refresh audit.
quotes.usage_notes: Documents the chronic dual-maintenance problem. Good for content about single source of truth for product pricing.

quotes.quote: There's no IT department at this company. We're lean mean.
quotes.usage_notes: Validates the mid-market ICP: no dedicated IT, lean teams, must self-serve on implementation. Relevant for ease-of-setup messaging.

quotes.quote: Who are our customers that have that product bought? I don't know. So it's hard for me to cross-sell market if I don't know who has what.
quotes.usage_notes: Marketing-specific pain caused by subscription data being locked in finance spreadsheets. Broadens the pain beyond finance to revenue teams. Strong for content about CRM data completeness.

deviant_terms.term: share the magic behind the curtain
deviant_terms.meaning: Making finance-only subscription data accessible to the broader organization
deviant_terms.frequency: used once but captures the core motivation for the entire evaluation

deviant_terms.term: too busy firing my bow and arrow to learn to use your machine gun
deviant_terms.meaning: Team is so consumed by manual processes they lack bandwidth to implement tools that would reduce manual work
deviant_terms.frequency: attributed to a colleague; clearly a shared organizational sentiment

deviant_terms.meaning: Periodic attempt to re-sync the HubSpot product library with the pricing spreadsheet. Has been tried multiple times and failed to stick.
deviant_terms.frequency: used once by RevOps lead; indicates a recurring failed process

deviant_terms.meaning: CFO's deliberate contract standardization to avoid partial-month co-terming complexity
deviant_terms.frequency: used twice; reflects a philosophy of operational simplicity

keyword_candidates.source: Core job-to-be-done: migrating subscription tracking from Excel into HubSpot. CFO wants non-finance teams to see ARR, upsells, and contract status in the CRM.
keyword_candidates.content_angle: How mid-market SaaS companies move subscription tracking from spreadsheets to HubSpot without losing data fidelity

keyword_candidates.source: Explicit feature requirement discussed at length. CFO needs mid-contract upsells prorated to contract anniversary in whole calendar months.
keyword_candidates.content_angle: How to co-term mid-contract upsells in HubSpot: the gap in native quoting and how to solve it

keyword_candidates.source: CFO explicitly said HubSpot's native quoting is 'okay' and was shocked it lacks contract management natively. Tried and abandoned native quoting.
keyword_candidates.content_angle: Where HubSpot's native CPQ breaks down: complex pricing, co-terming, and multi-year contracts

keyword_candidates.source: Company profile is the ICP archetype: growth-stage SaaS, complex pricing (AUM-based tiers, annual escalators, co-terms), outgrown spreadsheets but not enterprise enough for DealHub.
keyword_candidates.content_angle: Why mid-market SaaS companies need CPQ before they think they do (and what to look for)

keyword_candidates.source: Entire evaluation is driven by migrating from Excel-based quoting and subscription management into HubSpot. Both CFO and RevOps lead described the spreadsheet dependency.
keyword_candidates.content_angle: The spreadsheet-to-HubSpot migration playbook for quoting and subscription management

keyword_candidates.source: CFO asked specifically about renewal quotes with annual escalators. Wants to auto-generate renewal quotes at contract anniversary with compounding increases.
keyword_candidates.content_angle: Automating renewal quotes in HubSpot: annual escalators, co-termed upsells, and contract anniversary billing

keyword_candidates.source: Extended discussion about price books for AUM-based tiered pricing. RevOps lead asked detailed questions about how price books work in Quotivity vs. HubSpot.
keyword_candidates.content_angle: How to set up price books in HubSpot for tiered, volume, and segment-based pricing

keyword_candidates.source: CFO's primary motivation: making ARR and subscription data visible to non-finance teams inside HubSpot instead of locked in a spreadsheet.
keyword_candidates.content_angle: Why your CRM should be your ARR source of truth (and how to get there from spreadsheets)
